ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for double fish beehoon soup with milk; mapped to broader noodle soup food product class as closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singaporean double fish beehoon soup with evaporated milk; A0FJP (rice noodle soup with fish) used as closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific Singaporean dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre bowl of double fish beehoon soup with milk weighs approximately 500 g and contains a mix of fresh and deep-fried fish slices, thick rice vermicelli, vegetables, and a creamy evaporated-milk broth.

nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Double fish beehoon soup (with milk)', food code F05060044, with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli (rice noodles, boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for double fish beehoon soup with milk as a composite dish. GI estimated at 53 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: rice vermicelli (beehoon), which has a published GI of approximately 53 per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The protein from fish and fat from evaporated milk further attenuate the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (53 × 47.35 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 25.1, rounded to 25.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 282 mg per 100 g; a 500 g bowl delivers approximately 1,410 mg sodium, representing 61–94% of the recommended daily limit of 1,500–2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid consuming the full soup broth, request reduced soy sauce, and limit portion size to reduce sodium load from this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli (beehoon) is a refined carbohydrate with a GI of approximately 53; a standard 500 g bowl provides approximately 47 g available carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of ~25.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a reduced noodle portion, increase the fish and vegetable ratio, and avoid sweetened condiments to moderate post-prandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 282 mg per 100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at approximately 0.9 g per 100 g, and fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit soup broth consumption to reduce sodium intake, while the omega-3-rich fish component provides a beneficial anti-inflammatory effect on cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish provides omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) supporting joint inflammation reduction; evaporated milk contributes calcium for bone protection in patients on corticosteroids.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the omega-3 content of the fish; request reduced soy sauce to limit sodium-associated tissue swelling and omit fried shallots to reduce oxidised fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double Fish Beehoon Soup with Milk is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of both fresh and deep-fried sliced fish pieces served in a creamy, milky broth made with fish stock and evaporated milk, accompanied by thick rice vermicelli (beehoon) and leafy vegetables. The dish is characterised by its rich, savoury-sweet flavour profile derived from the evaporated milk, which gives the broth a pale, creamy appearance distinct from the clear-broth version. The fish is typically sliced threadfin (ikan kurau) or batang (Spanish mackerel), with the deep-fried pieces adding textural contrast. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 62 kcal, 2.74 g protein, 2.16 g fat, 9.47 g carbohydrate, 0.7 g sugar, 0.62 g dietary fibre, and 282 mg sodium. The evaporated milk contributes calcium and saturated fat, while the fish provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids. The glycaemic index is estimated at 53 (low) based on the rice vermicelli component, though a standard 500 g serving yields a glycaemic load of approximately 25, which is clinically significant for diabetes management. Sodium content is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, as a full bowl can deliver over 1,400 mg sodium. The dish is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, and is considered a lighter, lower-calorie noodle option compared to richer dishes such as laksa or prawn noodles.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F05060044) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate component (rice vermicelli) from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean hawker dish are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05060044 as of 2026; no supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a sufficiently close composite match to supply these values reliably. Saturated fat estimated at 0.9 g per 100 g based on the health_suitability note in the input record referencing approximately 0.9 g saturated fat per 100 g, consistent with the evaporated milk and deep-fried fish components. Cultural tags in the input record incorrectly classified this dish as 'western' and 'vegetarian/plant_based'; these have been corrected to Singaporean cuisine with appropriate dietary flags (contains fish and dairy, not vegetarian). The input record listed 'malaysia' as region_tag; this dish is a Singaporean hawker staple and has been classified accordingly. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to allowed enumeration values.
